words.shortDefinition: A way to create reusable components that can work with a variety of types.
words.simpleDefinition: Type variables (<T>) — placeholders for actual types. Write code once, works with many types. Array<string>, Array<number>. Type-safe flexibility.
words.longDefinition: Allows you to define a component (function, class, or interface) with a 'type variable' (like <T>) that acts as a placeholder for the actual type used. This provides type safety without sacrificing flexibility.
words.connector: Think of generics like function parameters — instead of passing values, pass types. One function, works with string or number.
words.techContext: We used a generic type for our API response wrapper so it can handle any data payload while still being fully typed.
words.exampleSentence: The Array<string> type is a perfect example of generics, as it tells TypeScript exactly what kind of objects the array can hold.
words.etymology: from Latin genericus ('belonging to a genus').

words.shortDefinition: A type that can be one of several types.
words.simpleDefinition: Type can be one of several — string | number, value is either string or number. Use pipe (|). Models 'or' relationships.
words.longDefinition: Defined using the pipe (|) symbol. A variable with a union type (e.g., string | number) can hold a value that is either a string or a number. This is useful for variables that can naturally hold multiple kinds of data.
words.connector: Think of union types like multiple choice — answer can be A or B or C, not all at once.
words.techContext: The 'status' variable uses a union type to only allow the strings 'loading', 'success', or 'error'.
words.exampleSentence: Union types allow us to model complex scenarios where a function might return a result or an error object.

words.shortDefinition: A powerful way to define the shape of an object.
words.simpleDefinition: Define object shape — what properties + types it must have. Extendable, implementable by classes. Contracts for objects.
words.longDefinition: Used to name and describe the properties and types that an object must have. Interfaces can be extended, implemented by classes, and are the primary way to define contracts within a TypeScript codebase.
words.connector: Think of interfaces like blueprints — defines what rooms (properties) the house (object) must have.
words.techContext: We defined a 'User' interface to ensure that every user object in the app has an 'id', 'email', and 'fullName'.
words.exampleSentence: The compiler gave me an error because my object didn't match the required interface—it was missing the 'password' field.

words.shortDefinition: The process where TypeScript automatically determines the type of a variable.
words.simpleDefinition: TS figures out types automatically — let x = 3, TS knows x is number. No explicit annotation needed. Less code, same safety.
words.longDefinition: Even if you don't explicitly provide a type, TypeScript can often figure it out based on the initial value assigned to the variable. This reduces the amount of code you have to write while still maintaining type safety.
words.connector: Think of type inference like autocorrect — you don't spell everything out, it fills in the gaps intelligently.
words.techContext: I didn't need to specify that 'isLoggedIn' was a boolean because TypeScript's type inference saw that I set it to 'false'.
words.exampleSentence: Type inference helps keep our code clean by not requiring explicit types for every single variable.
words.etymology: from 'type' + Latin inferre ('to bring in, conclude').

codeExample.code:
let x = 3; // TypeScript infers that x is a number
// x = "hello"; // Error: Type 'string' is not assignable to 'number'

words.shortDefinition: A way to create a new name for a type.
words.simpleDefinition: Create new name for type — type ID = string | number. Works for primitives, unions, tuples. Simplify complex types.
words.longDefinition: Uses the 'type' keyword. Unlike interfaces, type aliases can be used for primitives, unions, and tuples. They are often used to simplify complex union types or to give a descriptive name to a specific data structure.
words.connector: Think of type aliases like nicknames — ID is shorter than 'string | number', same meaning.
words.techContext: We created a 'Point' type alias to represent a simple coordinate object with 'x' and 'y' values.
words.exampleSentence: I used a type alias to combine several different union types into a single 'AppEvent' type.

words.shortDefinition: A type-safe counterpart to 'any'.
words.simpleDefinition: Type-safe any — anything assignable to it, but can't use without type guard. Forces validation before use. Safer than any.
words.longDefinition: Anything is assignable to 'unknown', but 'unknown' isn't assignable to anything but itself and 'any' without a type assertion or a type guard. It forces you to perform some check before using the value.
words.connector: Think of unknown like a locked box — you know something's inside, but must unlock (type guard) before using.
words.techContext: When parsing data from an external API, we first mark it as 'unknown' to force the developer to validate the structure before using it.
words.exampleSentence: Switching from 'any' to 'unknown' helped us catch several runtime errors where we were assuming a property existed that wasn't there.

words.shortDefinition: A type for values that will never occur.
words.simpleDefinition: Type for functions that never return — always throw error or infinite loop. Used in exhaustiveness checking (switch default).
words.longDefinition: Used as the return type for functions that always throw an error or have an infinite loop. It is also used in 'exhaustiveness checking' in switch statements to ensure all cases have been handled.
words.connector: Think of never like a black hole — goes in, nothing comes out (no return).
words.techContext: The 'default' case of our switch statement is assigned to a 'never' type to ensure we get a compiler error if a new union member is added.
words.exampleSentence: The function returns 'never' because it enters an infinite while loop that listens for system events.
words.etymology: from Middle English nevere ('at no time').

words.shortDefinition: An array with a fixed number of elements whose types are known.
words.simpleDefinition: Fixed-length array with known types per position — [string, number]. First is string, second is number. React useState returns tuple.
words.longDefinition: Allows you to express an array where the type of a fixed number of elements is known, but need not be the same. For example, you can represent a value as a pair of a string and a number.
words.connector: Think of tuples like ordered pairs in math — (x, y), exactly 2 elements, order matters.
words.techContext: We used a tuple for our coordinate system [number, number] to ensure that each point always has exactly two numeric values.
words.exampleSentence: The React 'useState' hook returns a tuple containing the state value and its setter function.
words.etymology: from Latin -plus ('fold'), as in quadruple, quintuple.

words.shortDefinition: A way to combine multiple types into one.
words.simpleDefinition: Combine multiple types with & — Person & Serializable has all properties from both. For mixins, combining interfaces.
words.longDefinition: Defined using the ampersand (&) symbol. An intersection type (e.g., Person & Serializable) contains all the properties of all the combined types. It is often used for mixins or combining multiple interfaces.
words.connector: Think of intersection types like Venn diagram overlap — has everything from A and everything from B.
words.techContext: I used an intersection type to create a 'LoggedInUser' type that has all the properties of a 'User' plus an 'authToken'.
words.exampleSentence: Intersection types are perfect for scenarios where you need an object to satisfy multiple different contracts at once.

words.shortDefinition: Types that represent a single, specific value.
words.simpleDefinition: Exact value types — not 'string' but exactly 'GET' or 'POST'. Combine into unions for string enums. Better autocomplete.
words.longDefinition: Instead of just saying a variable is a 'string', you can say it must be exactly 'GET' or 'POST'. Literal types are most powerful when combined into unions to create 'enums' of strings or numbers.
words.connector: Think of literal types like exact change — not 'some coins' but exactly '25 cents'.
words.techContext: We used a string literal union for the 'align' property of our button component, restricting it to 'left', 'right', or 'center'.
words.exampleSentence: Literal types provide much better autocomplete and error checking than plain strings.

codeExample.code:
type Direction = "North" | "South" | "East" | "West";
let move: Direction = "North";

words.shortDefinition: A way to tell the compiler 'trust me, I know what I'm doing'.
words.simpleDefinition: Override inferred type with 'as' — element as HTMLInputElement. No runtime check, purely compiler. Dangerous if wrong.
words.longDefinition: Uses the 'as' keyword. It allows you to override TypeScript's inferred type with a more specific one. It does not perform any runtime verification or data conversion; it's purely for the compiler.
words.connector: Think of type assertion like signing a waiver — 'I know what I'm doing, don't check me.'
words.techContext: I used a type assertion to tell the compiler that the generic DOM element was actually an HTMLInputElement so I could access the '.value' property.
words.exampleSentence: Be careful with type assertions; if you assert the wrong type, your code might crash at runtime even if it passes the compiler.

codeExample.code: const myCanvas = document.getElementById("main") as HTMLCanvasElement;

words.shortDefinition: Expressions that perform a runtime check that guarantees the type of a variable in some scope.
words.simpleDefinition: Runtime checks that narrow types — typeof, instanceof, custom 'is' functions. Narrows union to specific member.
words.longDefinition: Common type guards include 'typeof', 'instanceof', and custom user-defined functions using 'is'. They allow TypeScript to 'narrow' a union type to a more specific member of that union.
words.connector: Think of type guards like ID checks — verify it's a string before treating it as one.
words.techContext: We wrote a custom type guard to check if an object is a 'PremiumUser' before accessing its billing properties.
words.exampleSentence: The 'if' statement acted as a type guard, allowing me to treat the variable as a string inside the block.

words.shortDefinition: A pattern for narrowing types based on a common 'tag' property.
words.simpleDefinition: Tagged unions — common 'kind' property narrows type. {kind: 'circle'} vs {kind: 'square'}. Safe state machines, switch statements.
words.longDefinition: Also known as Tagged Unions. Every member of the union has a common property with a literal type (the 'discriminant'). This allows for clean and safe handling of complex state machines using switch statements.
words.connector: Think of discriminated unions like sorting mail — check 'type' label (discriminant), route to correct handler.
words.techContext: Our 'UIState' is a discriminated union with a 'kind' property that can be 'loading', 'empty', or 'content'.
words.exampleSentence: Discriminated unions make it impossible to accidentally access properties that only exist in the 'error' state when the app is in the 'success' state.

words.shortDefinition: Built-in types provided by TypeScript to facilitate common type transformations.
words.simpleDefinition: Built-in type helpers — Partial<T> (all optional), Pick<T, K> (select subset), Omit<T, K> (remove subset). Avoid repeating definitions.
words.longDefinition: Common examples include Partial<T> (makes all properties optional), Pick<T, K> (selects a subset of properties), and Omit<T, K> (removes a subset of properties). They help avoid repeating large interface definitions.
words.connector: Think of utility types like Photoshop filters — apply transformation (make optional, pick fields) to existing type.
words.techContext: We used the Omit utility type to create a 'UserUpdatePayload' that includes all user properties except for the 'id'.
words.exampleSentence: Using the 'Partial' utility type allowed us to use the same interface for both the initial user registration and the profile update form.

words.shortDefinition: A way to create new types by transforming each property in an existing type.
words.simpleDefinition: Transform every property in type — iterate with 'in keyof T', apply transformation. Foundation of Partial, Readonly. Auto-sync.
words.longDefinition: Uses the 'in' keyword to iterate over keys of an existing type. They are the foundation of many utility types. For example, you can create a type that makes every property in another type read-only or nullable.
words.connector: Think of mapped types like forEach for types — visit each property, transform it (make readonly, boolean).
words.techContext: I created a mapped type to automatically generate a set of boolean flags based on the keys of our feature config object.
words.exampleSentence: Mapped types are incredibly powerful for keeping related types in sync automatically as the source interface changes.

words.shortDefinition: Types that choose a different type based on a condition.
words.simpleDefinition: Type-level if-else — T extends U ? X : Y. Type depends on condition. Advanced type logic.
words.longDefinition: Follows the syntax 'T extends U ? X : Y'. They allow for complex logic within the type system, enabling types that depend on the input types of a function or the properties of an interface.
words.connector: Think of conditional types like ternary operators — condition ? ifTrue : ifFalse, but for types.
words.techContext: We used a conditional type to automatically determine if a function should return a single object or an array based on its arguments.
words.exampleSentence: Conditional types allowed us to build a highly flexible API client that provides perfect typing for varied return shapes.

words.shortDefinition: A way to create new string literal types by combining other types using template literal syntax.
words.simpleDefinition: String manipulation at type level — `hello ${World}` creates exact string type. Generate CSS classes, event names. TS 4.1+.
words.longDefinition: Introduced in TS 4.1. They allow you to define types like 'on-click' or 'on-change' by combining a list of events with a prefix. They provide powerful string manipulation capabilities at the type level.
words.connector: Think of template literal types like string interpolation — but builds types, not runtime values.
words.techContext: We used template literal types to automatically generate all possible CSS color classes (like 'bg-red-500') from our design token definitions.
words.exampleSentence: Template literal types allowed us to type our event emitter so it only accepts valid event names constructed from our action list.

words.shortDefinition: A special kind of declaration that can be attached to a class, method, or property.
words.simpleDefinition: Annotations that modify behavior — @Get, @Log. Metaprogramming for classes/methods/properties. NestJS, Angular.
words.longDefinition: Used to modify or annotate the behavior of the item they are attached to. They are a form of metaprogramming and are commonly used in frameworks like NestJS or Angular for dependency injection and routing.
words.connector: Think of decorators like gift wrapping — adds extra layer (@Log) around the original (method).
words.techContext: We used the '@Get' decorator on our controller method to define it as a handler for HTTP GET requests.
words.exampleSentence: I added a '@Log' decorator to the service method to automatically record the execution time of every call.

words.shortDefinition: Files ending in .d.ts that provide type information for existing JavaScript code.
words.simpleDefinition: .d.ts files — type definitions for JS libraries. No implementation, just types. @types/library-name. Enables type safety for JS.
words.longDefinition: They contain only type definitions, not implementation. They allow TypeScript to understand the structure of third-party JavaScript libraries so you can use them with full type safety and autocomplete.
words.connector: Think of declaration files like instruction manuals — describe what exists (types), don't build it (implementation).
words.techContext: Since the library was written in plain JS, I had to install its declaration file from @types/library-name to use it in our TS project.
words.exampleSentence: The declaration file for the legacy analytics script fixed all the 'implicitly has an any type' errors in the build.

words.shortDefinition: The process of converting TypeScript code into JavaScript code.
words.simpleDefinition: Convert TS to JS — tsc removes types, downgrades modern features for browser compatibility. Browsers can't run TS directly.
words.longDefinition: Because browsers and Node.js cannot run TypeScript directly, the TypeScript compiler (tsc) must 'transpile' it. This involves removing all type information and converting modern features into a version of JS the target environment supports.
words.connector: Think of transpilation like translation — TypeScript (French) to JavaScript (English), meaning preserved.
words.techContext: The transpilation step in our build process takes about 10 seconds and outputs clean ES2020 JavaScript.
words.exampleSentence: Transpilation allows us to use future JavaScript features today without worrying about browser compatibility.

words.shortDefinition: A type that essentially turns off type checking for a variable.
words.simpleDefinition: Turns off type checking — allows any value, any operation. Useful for JS migration, but anti-pattern. Use noImplicitAny.
words.longDefinition: Using 'any' allows you to assign any value to a variable and perform any operation on it. While useful for transitioning from JS, its overuse is considered an anti-pattern as it negates the benefits of using TypeScript.
words.connector: Think of any like turning off spell-check — convenient short-term, but defeats the purpose.
words.techContext: We enabled the 'noImplicitAny' rule to ensure that developers don't accidentally leave variables typed as 'any'.
words.exampleSentence: I had to use 'any' as a temporary measure while refactoring the complex legacy object that didn't have a clear interface yet.

words.shortDefinition: A configuration setting that enables a range of type-checking behaviors.
words.simpleDefinition: Enables strictest type checking — strictNullChecks, noImplicitAny. Most robust safety. tsconfig.json 'strict' flag.
words.longDefinition: Controlled by the 'strict' flag in tsconfig.json. It enables several sub-flags like strictNullChecks (prevents using null/undefined as values) and noImplicitAny, resulting in the most robust type safety.
words.connector: Think of strict mode like hard difficulty — catches more errors, harder to write code, safer result.
words.techContext: Enabling strict mode in our project helped us find dozens of potential null pointer exceptions before they reached production.
words.exampleSentence: The compiler error in strict mode forced me to handle the case where the user's email might be undefined.

words.shortDefinition: A way to tell the TypeScript compiler about variables that exist outside the current file.
words.simpleDefinition: Tell TS about global variables — declare var google, window.$. For globals from scripts, environment. Uses 'declare' keyword.
words.longDefinition: Uses the 'declare' keyword. Ambient declarations inform the compiler that a variable (like 'google' or '$') is provided by a global script or the environment, so it shouldn't throw an error for it being undefined.
words.connector: Think of ambient declarations like informing customs — 'this global exists, I didn't bring it, it's just here.'
words.techContext: I added an ambient declaration for the global 'analytics' object so we could call its methods without the compiler complaining.
words.exampleSentence: Ambient declarations are essential when working with legacy libraries that attach themselves to the global window object.

codeExample.code: declare var globalConfig: { apiKey: string };

words.shortDefinition: A way to look up a specific property type on another type.
words.simpleDefinition: Extract property type — User['id'] gives type of id field. Keeps related types in sync automatically.
words.longDefinition: Allows you to extract the type of a specific property from an interface or type alias using bracket notation (e.g., User['id']). This ensures that related variables always stay in sync with the source type.
words.connector: Think of indexed access types like object property access — but returns the type, not the value.
words.techContext: We used an indexed access type to ensure that our 'onUpdate' callback always receives the correct type for the 'userId' field.
words.exampleSentence: Indexed access types are useful for building dynamic components that need to know the type of a specific field in a model.

codeExample.code:
interface User { id: number; name: string; }
type IdType = User["id"]; // number

words.shortDefinition: The process of moving from a less specific type to a more specific type.
words.simpleDefinition: Refine type from broad to specific — string | null becomes string after if check. Result of type guards, truthiness checks.
words.longDefinition: It is the result of using type guards, literal equality, or truthiness checks. TypeScript uses these checks to refine the type of a variable within a specific branch of code, providing better safety and autocomplete.
words.connector: Think of type narrowing like zooming in — start wide (union), zoom to specific (string) based on checks.
words.techContext: TypeScript performed type narrowing after my 'if' check, allowing me to access string methods on the variable that was previously 'string | null'.
words.exampleSentence: Type narrowing is the magic that makes union types so easy and safe to work with in TypeScript.
